facts.text: Brooklyn Heights became a National Historic Landmark District in 1965, the first such district in New York City.
The neighborhood’s streets were laid out by Hezekiah Pierrepont in 1802, who sold plots to early developers and named many roads after prominent figures.
Brooklyn Heights’ first library opened in 1857, founded by the Mercantile Library Association of the City of Brooklyn.
The Brooklyn Historical Society occupies a Renaissance Revival building designed by George B. in 1878‑81, now a landmark in the Heights.
The Brooklyn Heights Promenade, a cantilevered esplanade over the BQE, was created in 1910 after community groups joined Moses in its design.
Brooklyn Heights was originally called Brooklyn Village and grew into a fashionable residential area after ferry service to Manhattan began in 1814.
